intro_en: Multi-agent systems are not valuable merely because they contain multiple agents. They become useful when context separation, specialization, and external feedback create new information or genuine parallelism. This section compares collaboration topologies, infrastructure, and team failure modes, then shows the model–Harness co-evolution flywheel on real tasks.

concepts.explanation_en: The central architectural decision is whether a new agent inherits the previous agent's full trajectory. Shared context discards no records during role transitions and simplifies short sequential handoffs, but it rapidly expands the context window and carries reasoning inertia forward; even a long context can still push details out of the model's attention. Non-shared context requires explicit interfaces, but provides modularity, failure isolation, parallelism, and focused attention for each role. With shared context, the system prompt and tool set change, so the agent's identity is new even though the history remains unified. The book's coding workflow transitions from Requirements Analyst to Software Engineer to Code Reviewer through dedicated transition tools, preserving requirement details and allowing rollback. Analytical extrapolation: Switch modes based on measured context occupancy, retrieval misses, and handoff-loss rate rather than a fixed threshold alone.
concepts.analogy_en: Shared context is a single meeting room where everyone has heard everything; isolation is a set of departments with their own offices, standardized forms, and handoff protocols.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Shared Context and Context Isolation

concepts.explanation_en: Topology determines where control resides and how information flows. A peer loop enables mutual review by a small pair; a Manager centralizes decomposition, planning, monitoring, and synthesis; decentralized handoff distributes routing decisions among specialized agents. Without shared context, communication must be expressed as a contract rather than a retelling of the entire trajectory. Proposer–Reviewer is suitable for iterative artifacts and passes structured feedback. A Manager treats sub-agents as tools: it sends a task and relevant context, receives conclusions, findings, artifact paths, and problems, and leaves full traces in local logs. In planner–executor tasks where an executor cannot easily compensate for decomposition errors, the chapter recommends prioritizing the strongest model and best prompt for the Planner or Manager. Analytical extrapolation: Describe the topology as a machine-readable graph with ownership, permitted transitions, and completion conditions.
concepts.analogy_en: A peer topology is two editors working on the same text; a Manager is a project office; decentralized handoff is a relay in which each participant chooses the next specialist based on the situation.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Peer, Manager, and Decentralized Collaboration Topologies

concepts.explanation_en: The book's criterion is that multi-agent collaboration has substantive value when it introduces a new signal—an execution result, screenshot, external source, or independent measurement. Reconsidering the same text under an equal compute budget often merely transfers and loses information. Additional agents and steps help only when the system has an explicit budget-allocation strategy; this is a design heuristic, not a universal law. Self-review without external feedback can reduce accuracy; CRITIC loses most of its gains when tool-based verification is removed. On WebGen-Bench, WebGen-Agent with visual feedback raised Claude 3.5 Sonnet's score from 26.4% to 51.9%. Debate over the same text has no guaranteed advantage when the budget of thinking tokens is held equal. Analytical extrapolation: Compare variants by quality per dollar, p95 latency, and marginal gain per additional agent-step.
concepts.analogy_en: Five experts rereading the same memo do not create new facts; one laboratory test changes the situation because it contributes a new observation.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Information Gain and Budget-Aware Scaling

concepts.explanation_en: An isolated multi-agent system requires two infrastructure layers independent of the chosen topology. The data plane moves durable artifacts through a virtual filesystem with distinct visibility scopes, lifecycles, and access permissions. The control plane routes messages, stores task state, and supports graceful or forced termination. A2A standardizes capability discovery, task lifecycles, and artifact exchange across trust boundaries, but does not replace internal parameters, files, or the message bus. The virtual filesystem mounts each agent's private scratchpad, a persistent shared workspace, permission-bound external resources, and stable read-only system resources. A path acts as a lightweight reference instead of loading content into context. Analytical extrapolation: Write messages and state transitions to an append-only trace with correlation IDs for replay, investigation, and cross-organization audits.
concepts.analogy_en: The data plane is a freight network with different warehouses, the control plane is a dispatch center with status indicators and stop signals, and A2A provides the international waybills and border-crossing rules.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Data and Control Planes with A2A Interoperability

concepts.explanation_en: Multi-agent systems introduce system, alignment, and verification failures, along with two amplifiers: concurrent shared state and semantic cascades. Local consistency does not prove truth; one initial error can pass through several specialized roles and acquire false authority. Protection combines isolation, concurrency control, dependency validation, and independent chain breakers. MAST distinguishes Specification and System Design Failures, Inter-Agent Misalignment, and Task Verification and Termination. Optimistic locking on a version catches a lost update to one file, while coding agents are better isolated in separate Git worktrees until merge. A cross-file conflict—for example, renumbered illustrations with stale references—requires dependency tracking and a global consistency check. Analytical extrapolation: Build a provenance graph from outputs to claims so that invalidating one source automatically flags the entire dependent cascade.
concepts.analogy_en: An error is like an incorrect unit of measurement in the first blueprint: every subsequent department can follow the document perfectly and still scale the defect across the entire structure.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Failure Containment and Independent Cross-Validation

concepts.explanation_en: Long-running, unconstrained interaction produces social, economic, and strategic effects that do not appear in local task orchestration. At the same time, the model and its agent Harness form a co-evolution flywheel: real tasks reveal model boundaries, external logic temporarily compensates for them, collected failures become training signals, and the stronger model moves the Harness to the next capability frontier. In Smallville, 25 generative agents use memory, reflection, and planning; an invitation to a party spreads without a central script. The book presents Moltbook as a reported example of agent network culture and, in the described Vending-Bench Arena runs, reports price wars and proposals to coordinate prices; the scale and degree of autonomy require separate verification. Analytical extrapolation: Maintain a map from each Harness constraint to its supporting evaluation, training candidate, and safe-removal criterion, and separately assess emergent norms, collusion, and risks to humans.
concepts.analogy_en: A Harness is scaffolding around a building that keeps growing: completed floors are freed from it, but the scaffolding moves upward, while the construction log helps improve the next structure.
concepts.visual_title_en: Visual Model: Agent Society and Model–Harness Co-Evolution
